transcript.text: This is the funnel that my company uses
to generate millions of dollars in
topline revenue per month. But it's also
the same exact funnel that we've used
for our clients to generate hundreds of
millions of dollars of enterprise value.
And if you've ever considered selling
something online, I strongly recommend
you use this exact structure because
it's going to convert basically out the
gate on day one. Now, it's called a
funnel because it takes large amounts of
views or leads through multiple stages
of filters, removes the lower quality
leads, and then drives the high quality
ones to an eventual conversion event at
the very bottom. Now, most client
acquisition processes or funnels have
one of two issues. There's not enough
leads, so simply getting more leads
would fix the business, or an issue
converting the leads, so converting more
leads would fix the business. Now, my
company specializes in getting leads
through social media, but converting
those leads is what this funnel is all
about. And after working directly with
hundreds of businesses, I realized one
of the biggest reasons more companies
don't have this figured out is that
there's almost no resources online about
this stuff, at least not at the multiple
million dollar per month level. So that
is what this video covers. So, if you
have an existing funnel that isn't
bringing you enough sales despite having
some leads, or if you're scaling past
100,000 a month and you need to optimize
or your cost per lead is too high and
your lead quality is too low, I will
walk you through our funnel in the exact
order our clients experience it so you
can just copy it. And even better, I'll
show you the specific decisions we made
and why we made them. So, instead of
just copying us, you can understand how
to interpret the data and how that can
apply to your business and create
something that is truly custom for your
business. business and therefore far
more effective than any cookie cutter
solution ever will be. If you're working
with our team, we'll build this out for
you. But you should understand how and
why we do this stuff so that you can
make the most of it. And so here is the
high-level flow for how we take leads
from traffic to sales call. So we start
with that traffic source, whether it's
organic or paid. Then we go to the
application page, which is a direct
offer. There's no opt-in requiring you
to actually see the page. It loads as
soon as you click in. There's an
application element which includes
friction or routing. There's the booking
page that comes after that which allows
you to select a time on the calendar.
There's a pre-all page. Once you've
selected the time on the calendar, this
pre-all page sets expectations for that
call. Then there's a pre-all sequence
that happens through a function of
multiple different automations. It's
reminders as well as qualification
processes before the call. And then we
have economics of calendar availability
which really influences who gets on the
sales call which is the very last step.
The discovery and close happens on the
sales call and that's when you actually
make the sale. So starting with the
traffic source, we have multiple
different funnels for different types of
traffic sources. So we can track lead
quality and quantity coming from each.
For example, we have identical funnels
built out separately for meta ads, one
for Google ads, one for Instagram
organic, one for YouTube organic, one
for email conversions. And it's really
important to track all of these
separately because for every traffic
source, we have different metrics. For
example, organic leads that come to us
through my Instagram organic or my
YouTube, they convert three times better
than traffic from paid ads. And so if
you blend the organic and paid into one
funnel, you can't tell which traffic
source is driving revenue for your
business. You can't tell which leads are
more qualified than others. And so
you're going to make bad strategic
decisions. We actually did this in our
first few months scaling this business.
And at the time, I thought more leads
would mean more sales. But it turns out
60% of our revenue at the time was
coming from organic traffic. So when I
doubled our ad spend and then I doubled
it again from 50 to over 500,000 per
month, we scaled it really quickly. All
it did was put us in a ton of debt
because we weren't making sales from
that cold traffic. We were doing
$700,000 a month topline at the time and
within 3 months I was almost personally
completely bankrupt. Fun fact, track the
source of your leads. And worst case,
like bare minimum, at least have one
funnel for organic and one funnel from
paid because if your organic and your
paid are working, you need to keep them
separate. Now, the very first page is
the application page. Once someone
clicks through either from an ad or from
organic content, they land on our
application page. And this page is made
of just a couple of parts. Starting
right at the top, we've got the brand
name so they know what page this is. Got
a quick little plug showing a
reputation. And then we've got a
headline. sometimes a video depending on
the page, a button to apply, and then
the actual application, which is what
the whole page is about, and then a
little further down here is some case
studies, some testimonials for people
who are a little bit more skeptical or
want more information on what we
actually do. That's it. There's no
complex layout and it's ideally not a
million different words on the screen
that no one reads. Anyways, we've tested
hundreds of iterations of this. We've
put millions of dollars behind this page
testing with ads. This is what works
best for our B2B audience. What works
for your audience might be different.
But just to prove to you that this works
for B2B audiences, I wanted to show you
a couple other pages that also make
millions of dollars per month and have
come to the same optimized page
structure. The first is Alex Herozy's
page promoting his workshops. He's got
the big headline, he's got the video
right down here, and then he's got a
button that goes to the application, and
they're asking for information there.
Next, Contrarian Thinking by Cody
Sanchez. You've got the brand name.
You've got the headline. Few words here,
but not too many. You've got the video
that they can choose to watch. And then
you've got a button to book your call,
which brings you to the application. And
then Hyros, they've got an even
simplified version of this. A very quick
headline, book a five minute prove it
demo collecting information right down
here. And then they've got a few
testimonials down there. And of course,
the logo so that you know what page
you're on. Now, this headline on all of
these websites is the most important
piece of copywriting in the entire
business because when people land on
this page, they need to know exactly
what they get from working with you.
This is your entire offer condensed into
just one or two lines. So, we for
example offer multiple services. We do
staffing, we do team development,
advisory services, one-on-one delivery,
we do some group stuff, we do
educational material, AI software, media
buying, video, graphics, all the stuff.
But for the headline, we're only
focusing on the one thing that sells the
best right now. Getting all your content
done quickly. Or we have another one
that we're split testing. 1 million
views or you don't pay. Very short. And
this spot on your website should have
the most important line of text in your
entire business. What can you say in as
few words as possible that will convince
people to work with you? Next on this
page, we have the VSSL, the video sales
letter. It's not always necessary. We
currently run traffic to pages with and
without a VSSL. And if you run a
business with clear deliverables like
landscaping or cosmetic medical
services, the results that you've gotten
for clients like a before and after is
going to communicate way more value than
you can on video. So you can feel free
to skip this step. But there are some
industries in which it makes sense to
test a video sales letter such as
coaching or consulting or marketing or
video production where the person on
camera has to first build trust with the
audience for their coaching or their
expertise to be valuable. And so I would
always test without a VSSL before adding
a VSSL just because making a VSSL and
actually executing well enough for them
to be worth the effort is really
difficult. Now within the VSSL there is
actually a structure to it. Ideally in
the first 15 seconds you explain what
you do and why people benefit from it.
You want to make this very much for the
benefit of the person watching the
video. In the next 15 seconds you want
to explain why this is better or
different than what they've tried before
because they've probably heard a similar
sales pitch in the past. and you need to
be able to differentiate yourself. The
next 15 seconds you use to provide proof
or client testimonials or case studies,
some kind of evidence that what you do
actually works. The next 15 seconds
remind them what they get and give a
clear CTA to do the next step. And then
the rest of the VSSL, another 3, 5, 10
minutes are just the most important
parts that you want your prospects to
hear based on what they respond best to
on sales calls. All of the words or
examples or word tracks that you use on
sales calls that get great responses
from your prospects that will tell you
what your prospects are looking for.
Just include them here in the video word
for word. Make sure you can use the best
parts about what you do and how you sell
it specifically as it's recognized by
your audience in that video. Now, for
length of the VSSL, we've tested
multiple lengths. When coming from
social media, usually around the 2 and
1/2 to 3 minute mark is where most
people stop watching. Their attention
span starts to falter. So, we've
condensed all the critical information
into that window. And then we've added
another 2 minutes on top of that for the
more skeptical or curious prospects.
going through the highlights of our
offer, the best parts, the things from
thousands of sales calls that we get the
most responses from from prospects, like
addressing common objections, so on
budget and authority and needs and
timing, reinforcing value as to like why
they should actually do this or why it's
so important, and then also comparing
our solution to others. And we always
wrap the video up, the very last thing
we say on the VSSL is one last call to
action for what you want them to do
next. For example, to complete the
application below the video sales
letter. Now, if you want to test a VSSL,
I made a GPT agent based on all of these
best practices that'll help you write
one yourself. It's linked down below. It
comes with a little instruction. It'll
walk you through it. Super easy. But
then we have the application itself. And
then the whole rest of the page is just
testimonials and proof that we can
deliver on what we do. And we found
through testing that no amount of proof
beats promise. like no crazy guarantees
beat hundreds of testimonials that you
can flood your potential clients with.
We've tested very bold guarantees and
promises on the front end, but
testimonials almost always convert
better. And if you don't have
testimonials yet, first, why would you
sell something that you don't have proof
works, but second, you probably do have
proof that it works. Like, let's be
real, sometimes at the start, the proof
is your own result, your own
testimonial, what you were able to
accomplish for yourself or maybe at
another company. And you should spend
the time it takes to organize that proof
into one easy place like this for
potential clients to come and just
understand which will make it easier for
them to give you money. Now getting into
the actual application once someone
clicks see if you qualify they get
pushed to this part of the page. When
designing the application we pay
attention to two things. One is friction
and one is routing. So first the
friction. The amount of friction either
increases or decreases lead cost by
making it harder or easier for someone
to apply. For example, we ask their
monthly revenue in their business. This
adds friction. You are making them
disclose personal information. Some
people won't do it. So your lead cost is
going to get significantly more
expensive just by adding this question.
But the people that do disclose their
income are probably less skeptical and
distrusting. uh more open to discussing
personal information like income which
makes it easier to sell them. So asking
that question will probably increase the
quality of your leads despite making
them more expensive. That is friction.
And the second thing is routing. So
routing allows you to give your best
leads special treatment based on how
they answer. For example, from our
application, people that are making
under 10,000 a month are routed to free
training. Like we just don't have a
product for them at this time. Totally
understand. Here's free material to help
you out. People with 10 to 50k a month
in income are lower quality leads that
are routed to specific sales reps that
have more time to dedicate to each one.
50k plus in income, that's around a
million a year topline. Those are higher
quality leads generally routed to our
most experienced sales reps to have
higher level business conversations. And
250k plus leads, so those are the
highest quality, flagged by our system
accordingly and treated very specially.
Now, every single answer that they
submit here sends them to a different
page after they click book. And each
page has a different tracking pixel
installed. And this is very important
for pixel optimization. The goal if
you're running Facebook ads and
specifically Facebook ads tracking pixel
as well as most other advertisers pixel
is to give you the cheapest qualified
leads possible. And the problem is the
vend diagram between the cheapest leads
and the worst leads has massive overlap.
It is like a circle. And the highest
quality leads are often the most
expensive. So the advertisers aren't
incentivized to give you the highest
quality leads. They're incentivized to
give you the cheapest ones. And so by
adding friction and by adding routing,
we can control what Facebook recognizes
as a positive result as positive
feedback. We can control what we tell
Facebook to get more of. We want to tell
Facebook to only get people like this
when they're rich, when they're
qualified, when they want to start right
now. For our example, we're always
changing and messing around with our
lead routing and lead friction depending
on marketing costs and our sales team
availability. But right now, we only
take on calls if they make a certain
amount of revenue. If they demonstrate a
certain amount of marketing ability in
the last 30 days to spend, have a high
enough client average lifetime value,
which at the higher end often reflects
higher level service-based businesses,
and businesses that are ready to start
on social media right now as opposed to
ones that are kind of just price
shopping and looking around. In a visual
analogy for this, if you imagine
Facebook is just throwing darts blindly
without any friction or routing, every
single time a dart connects with
something, Facebook is going to think it
did a good thing. But with friction and
routing, you can put up a dart board and
say, "Only give me leads that hit this
dart board that they're rich and they're
qualified." Or even better, say, "Only
give me leads that hit this bullseye,"
which is my perfect type of client. But
you can only do that when you have
friction and routing. So, if your leads
are either too expensive or too low
quality, those are the two levers you
can pull. Friction to add or remove
qualifying questions or routing, sending
different leads to different pages so
that you only give pixel data from the
positive results. You should test both,
track the data, and adjust as needed. As
always, now after someone submits their
application, they get routed to a
booking page where they can select a
time for their call. We always book two
to seven days in advance, sometimes
eight or nine, never multiple weeks out
in advance because if people book out
multiple weeks out in advance, the show
rates drop dramatically for our type of
prospect. They lose interest in what we
do or they don't even show up to the
call altogether. And if you're selling
to consumers and not organized business
owners like we do, you could expect that
window to be even shorter. So, it might
only be a couple of days. Or in home
service, for example, you will make way
more money if you can take calls same
day or ideally within the same hour that
the prospect requires. is if you've got
someone calling you because their boiler
broke down, they're probably not going
to want a call back in 7 days because
after a couple hours, they would have
called someone else to fix the issue and
your revenue per inbound lead is going
to drop. Now, once they select their
time and book in, they enter a very
robust email and SMS sequence. Honestly,
it's absurd. I'm not going to bother you
with the whole thing. Our tech person
spent months building this out there.
Over a 100 automations for all kinds of
unique sequences and triggers. Not all
of that is necessary if you're just
starting out, but after getting to
millions of dollars in monthly revenue,
we have had plenty of time and resources
dedicated to making this very
comprehensive. If you're just starting
out, something simple will work. If
you're working with us, don't worry.
Like, we'll set this up for you based on
what you need. Don't try to build it out
yourself. It's not worth the time. But
if we aren't setting this up for you,
here's what loosely works so you can
just take the best parts and copy that.
Immediately after booking a call, within
5 minutes, they should get a
confirmation email with a link to that
call so they can actually attend it and
an intro text from the sales rep saying,
"Hey, this is me. I'm the person you're
going to be talking to. Quick question
for you." And the question is to qualify
them. Ideally, you would also have an
introductory call with an assistant. So,
this can be an appointment setter or
receptionist that confirms the call was
booked at the right time and gathers
even more information about the
business. So the sales rep can show up
very prepared to the sales call with
tons of material prepped. And then after
that instant contact, we have a few
other sequences for automations. We have
24 hours before the call, an email
reminder with the link. 3 hours before
the call, a text reminder with a link
from the salesperson. 1 hour before the
call, an email reminder with the link
again. And then 30 minutes before, one
final text with the link. An important
note here is we alternate between phone
and email and text to reach every single
prospect with their preferred
communication. So like no matter what
that communication style they prefer is,
we've reached out with it. And we send
all of these reminders not just to
remind people but also to internally
measure how good and qualified of a lead
they are because the best leads give us
the most attention. And just some data
from a few hundred closes. People who
don't open an email or don't respond to
a text, they're completely cold. They
only have a 3% chance of showing up and
being qualified for the sale. People who
don't respond to text but don't open
email, 16%. People who open email but
don't respond to text, 14%, and then the
people who open the email with the link
to attend the call and respond to the
text from the sales rep getting more
information have a 42% chance to show up
and be qualified. This is one way we
measure how hot a lead is before we even
talk to them. And we move those less
qualified leads from a good sales rep's
calendar to a less experienced sales
rep's calendar so they can really work
that lead and make sure they show up and
make sure that they are qualified or
even to an assistant to take and
pre-qualify that lead before rebooking
them to an actual person if they are
qualified. This way, my best sales reps
only spend time talking to the most
qualified prospects. And at the end of
the day, I'm trying to get our best
sales reps the most amount of the most
qualified leads possible every single
day and squeeze as much efficiency out
of them as possible. Now, is this
pre-all sequence overkill? Maybe. If
you're scheduling everyone one day out
in advance and hitting with like six
reminders, it's possible. We've tested
dozens of variations of this, though,
and this is what maximize show rate
without being annoying to the point
where we lost revenue. So, as soon as a
prospect seems legit and confirms the
call, we also take a backseat and we
become a little bit more passive with
the reminders. But until that prospect
confirms and until we are confident that
they will show up and be qualified, we
are very prompt about getting a hold of
them because if we can't get a hold of
that lead, we are going to cancel the
call. We're going to have to give that
hour of my sales rep's time to a more
qualified or at least a more responsive
prospect. And now your audience might be
different like especially if you're B
toC, you might need to bug them a little
bit more. You might need to respond
quicker. and it's just the frequency and
style that we've found works best for
our business with our cold audience
specifically serving a B2B market
responding to an ad that they saw
online. If your audience or demographic
is different, test and track your own
numbers as always. This is just what
works for us. And then all of this
process leads down to a sales call,
which is where the conversion actually
happens. This funnel is not fancy. The
VSSL isn't highly produced. It's not
like a corporate training video, but it
works. and it actually potentially works
better for our market because of how
it's done. We've put tens of millions of
dollars in ads testing it. We could have
spent $100,000 doing the video. We've
tested hundreds of variations, but
simple sells surprisingly well. And so
that's what we keep doing. I have
multiple friends doing three 10 million
a month topline with their landing
pages, even more simple than ours. It's
literally a blank white page in a video.
It's surprising. And I think the lesson
that I want more people to take away
from here is that it's not the fanciest
page that wins. It's the one that
converts the best. And if you haven't
had millions of dollars in topline
revenue experience with landing pages, I
recommend you at least give this a try.
If you want to disagree with our best
practices, be my guest. Your hunch may
work better for your industry. But I
encourage you, please save yourself some
money and at least test a couple of
variations. And I think this one in
particular is very much worth your time.
And after testing, just let the data
decide. And if you like this video, this
next video is actually probably going to
be a lot more helpful because it'll
actually show you how to convert those
people once you've got them on a call
with you, which is the whole point of
this thing anyways.
